---
title: Data Products
---

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple" style="color: #d32f2f; border-left-color: #d32f2f; background-color: #ffebee;"}
## Improvements for this page
* add figures
* verify equations
:::

# Overview

Hydrophone data is archived several formats, ranging from raw acoustic recordings to processed spectral products.

Each device has a collection of files for every 5 minutes of recording:

| Description |  Data Product Code | Data Product ID | File Extension |
|---------|-----|---|---|
Raw Audio Data (lossless) |  AD | 7 | .flac
Raw Audio Data (lossless)| AD | 7 | .wav 
Raw Audio Data (with loss) | AD | 7 | .mp3
Raw Audio Data (IOS arrays only) | AD | 38 | .hyd (*retired*)
Proprietary Spectral Data (Ocean Sonics only) | HSD  | 45 | .fft
Processed Spectral Data  | HSD | 45 | .mat
Spectrogram | HSD | 45 | .png
Spectral Probability Density | HSPD | 51 | .png

: Archived formats for hydrophone data.

\

Note that both **Data Product Code** and **File Extension** are needed when downloading data through the API.
Each data product (dataProductCode) has a set of data product options (dpo) that can be use to narrow down the request.

::: {.callout-note}
## API Request Example

<pre style="font-family: 'Courier New', Courier, monospace; background: transparent; border: none; color: inherit;">
params = {
    'dateFrom': '2026-04-14T10:00:00.000Z',
    'dateTo': '2026-04-14T11:00:00.000Z',
    'deviceCode': 'ICLISTENHF6016', # Clayoquot Slope Hydrophone A
    'dataProductCode': 'HSD',        # Hydrophone Spectral Data
    'extension': 'mat',             # MATLAB file extension
    'dpo_resample': 'none',         # 'none' for full resolution
}
request = onc.orderDataProduct(params)
</pre>

:::


# Audio Data (AD)

Formats: .flac/.wav/.mp3

Available API data product options:

* dpo_audioDownsample: "16000", "8000", "256", "-1", "44100", "4000", "512", "2000", "48000"
* dpo_audioFormatConversion: "0", "1"
* dpo_audioFormatConversion: "1"
* dpo_hydrophoneAcquisitionMode: "All", "LF", "HF"
* dpo_hydrophoneChannel: "All", "H1", "H2", "H3"
* dpo_hydrophoneDataDiversionMode: "All", "LPF", "OD", "HPF"

The audio data is what is being recorded from the acoustic environment, it is the raw data.
Older data was stored in .wav format, but ONC has now fully transitioned to FLAC (Free Lossless Audio Codec), which allow for metadata to also be stored within the file.
It is possible to request the data in mp3 format, which has a much smaller file size, although not recommended for scientific research since it is a compressed format with several frequencies being lost.

Here a few advantages of the FLAC format:

* **Lossless Compression**: Unlike MP3s, FLAC retains 100% of the original information.
* **Storage Efficiency**: FLAC files are significantly smaller than .wav files and maintain the same quality.
* **Metadata Support**: FLAC files support metadata tagging for deployment and device information.

# Hydrophone Spectral Data (HSD)

Available extensions: .fft/.mat

Available API data product options:

* dpo_hydrophoneAcquisitionMode: "All", "LF", "HF"
* dpo_hydrophoneChannel: "All", "H1", "H2", "H3"
* dpo_hydrophoneDataDiversionMode: "All", "LPF", "OD", "HPF"
* dpo_lowerColourLimit: range from -160 to 140
* dpo_upperColourLimit: range from -160 to 140
* dpo_spectrogramColourPalette: "0", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5"
* dpo_spectrogramConcatenation: "Adjacent", "Daily", "Weekly", "None"
* dpo_spectrogramFrequencyUpperLimit: range from 100 to 500000
* dpo_spectrogramSource: "WAVFLAC", "FFT", "MIX"


Spectral products are advantageous for research of monitoring, because they allow for analyzing data without having to process terabytes of raw audio.

## Proprietary Spectral Data (.fft)

The Fast Fourier Transform files (.fft) are broadband spectrogram, and somewhat a raw version of the actual spectrogram.
They are unique to Ocean Sonics hydrophones.

Technical warning: the .fft files are usually considered "preliminary" and not suitable for scientific research, because a single broadband sensitivity value (around -165 to -170 dB) was used rather than a ferquency-dependent calibration curve.
They are best used for a "quick-look" to verify the hydrophone is functional.

Ocean Sonics has they in-house software *Lucy* to read the files, but it is also possible to read them with in python or MATLAB.

## Processed Spectral Data (.mat)

The MATLAB files contain the true scientific data where the **frequency-dependent sensitivity curve** has been applied.

* **Calibration**: Unlike .fft files, .mat spectral products are corrected using the specific hydrophone's calibration data.
* **Structure**: The data contain a SpectData MATLAB structure with frequency bins (Hz) and power spectral density.

## Spectrogram (.png)

A spectrogram is a 2D *heat map* of sound, visualizing how the **Power Spectral Density** of an acoustic signal evolves over time.

* X-axis: Time
* Y-axis: Frequency (Hz)
* Color/intensity: Sound Level expressed in $\text{dB}~\text{re}~1~\mu Pa^2 / Hz$.

Here is how a spectrogram is generated:

To transform raw audio into a visual spectrogram, the data undergoes several steps:

1. **Time Windowing**: Raw audio is divided into short, finite segments using a time window (e.g., Hann Window), to prevent spectral leakage.
2. **FFT Calculation**: A Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is applied to each segment, converting the signal from the *time domain* to the *frequency domain*.
3. **Averaging**: Successive windows are overlapped (typically by 50%) to ensure a smooth transition and prevent data loss at the window edges.
4. **Calibration**: The values are converted to physical units ($\mu$ Pa) by applying the device-specific frequency-dependent **calibration curve** (this step is critical!).

[!IMPORTANT]
There has been some issues in the past from using the wrong calibration curve: https://internal.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/ONCData/pages/255101693/1.+Power+spectral+density+vs.+power+spectrum.)

**Why use Windows?**

Fourier transforms assume a signal in *infinite*.
By analyzing finite segments, at the edges of each segment there is a discontinuity that causes **spectral leakage**, where a signal energy *leaks* into neighboring frequency bins.
**Windowing** (or *tapering*) solves this by multiplying the data segment by a specific mathematical shape (see examples below) that smooths the edges to zero.
Mathematically, this is what is called a **convolution**.
The Fourier transform of the convolution is the product of the individual Fourier transforms of the signal and the window.

**The Stationary Assumption**

Fourier transforms assume a signal is and *stationary*.
The Heisenberg Uncertainty Principe dictates that you cannot have perfect resolution in both time and frequency simultaneously.

* **Narrow window**: excellent **time resolution** (you see exactly when a whale click happened), but poor frequency resolution (the pitch is blurry).
* **Wide window**: excellent **frequency resolution** (you see the exact harmonics of a whale call), but poor time resolution (start and end times are blurry).


Time windowing:
*Windowing* is used to reduce spectral leakage when analyzing signals with a fourier transform.
Multiplying a spectral segment by a window smooths the segment edges and recues discontinuities that cause leakage.
Effectivemely, a segment is created by multiplying the data by a window of finitie length and a specific shape. This is called a *convolution*.
The window that multiplies the function is called the window function (or sometimes tapering function).
The Fourier transform of the convolution is the product of th eindividual Fourier transforms.
There's an implicit assumption that the signal frequency is invariant within the window, since it can only characterize stationay signals.
Therefore, the signal is decomposed into finite segments (each segment is assumed *stationary*), and for each time window the Fourier transform is applied.
If one slides the time-filtering window, then the signal is assumed to be decomposed with both information in frequency and time.
Narrow window leads to goop time resolution, wide window leads to good frequency resolution.
Can't have both: Heisenberg Unertainty Principle.
A drawback is that any signal with a wavelength longer than the window width is completely lost.
In contrast, longer windows retain more frequency components, but at the expense of losing the time resolution of the signal.

I found a similar past issue here: DSO-598

Examples of windows: 

In this example, the information of the spectrogram is as follows:

* Hann window with 50% overlap at ONC
* 128000 samples per FFT window
* Temporal resolution: 0.05 s
* Spectral resolution: 10 Hz

# Hydrophone Spectral Probability Density (HSPD)

Format: .png

Available API Data Product Options:

* dpo_filePlotBreaks: Full Range: "0", Hourly: "1", Daily: "2", Weekly: "3", Monthly: "4", Yearly: "5"
* dpo_hydrophoneAcquisitionMode: "All", "LF", "HF"
* dpo_hydrophoneChannel: 'All', "H1", "H2", "H3"
* dpo_hydrophoneDataDiversionMode: "All", "LPF", "OD", "HPF"
* dpo_spectralProbabilityDensityColourAxisUpperLimit:"0", "0.1", "1.0", "0.05", "0.25", "0.5", "0.12"
* dpo_spectralProbabilityDensityPSDRange: "0", "40_160", "40_140", "20_140"

This data product is a sophisticated statistical tool used for long-term ambient noise analysis.
It was originally proposed by [Merchant et al., (2013)](https://pubs.aip.org/asa/jasa/article/133/4/EL262/917238/Spectral-probability-density-as-a-tool-for-ambient).
It calculates how frequently a specific dB level occurs at each frequency bin.
The result is a heatmap showing the persistent ambiant noise floor

Derivation:

The mean square value in frequency range $(f, \Delta f)$ is:

$$
\Psi^2_x(f,\Delta f) = \lim_{T\rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T x(t,f,\Delta f)^2 dt
$$

The power spectral density is approximately $\Psi^2_x(f,\Delta f) \approx G_x(f) \Delta f$.

$$
G_x(f) = \lim_{\Delta f \rightarrow 0} \frac{\Psi^2_x(f,\Delta f)}{\Delta f} = \lim_{\Delta f\rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\Delta f} \left[ \lim_{T\rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T x(t,f,\Delta f)^2 dt \right]
$$

# Metadata & Log Files

## Oceans3.0: Hydrophone Auxiliary Data
Ocean Sonics devices provide a .txt file with *auxiliary* data, which are all the data related to the instrument itself, not the passive acoustics.
For example, information about internal humidity, temperature, battery charge, etc., are contained within this product.
Although the auxiliary data does not give any information regarding the ocean soudscape, it can be useful to diagnose potential issues, such as water leakage within the hydrophone (>50% humidity), overheating, or dead sensor (dead battery).
There are many more variables like magnetic flux and acceleration which can be used to determine the hydrophone direction and strong currents.

More information here: [insert link].

# Future Data Products

Visit the Passive Acoustics Data Products confluence page to find out ideas for future development of data products at ONC, and even add your own!
https://internal.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/ONCData/pages/216205790/Passive+acoustics

Read this for Spectral probability density as a tool for ambient noise analysis : https://pubs.aip.org/asa/jasa/article/133/4/EL262/917238/Spectral-probability-density-as-a-tool-for-ambient

# Useful links

```{=html}
<div style="border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.2); 
            border-radius: 8px; 
            padding: 16px; 
            background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.05); 
            margin-top: 15px;
            backdrop-filter: blur(5px);
            color: white;">

  <div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 12px; margin-bottom: 12px;">
    <img src="../images/confluence_logo_no_bckg.png" width="24" style="object-fit: contain;">
    <span style="font-weight: bold; font-size: 1.1em; letter-spacing: 0.3px;">
      Confluence Documentation
    </span>
  </div>

  <ul style="list-style-type: none; padding-left: 36px; margin: 0;">
    

	<li style="margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <a href="https://internal.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/DS/pages/7639165/Hydrophone+Matlab+Postprocess+Jobs" 
         style="color: #82caff; text-decoration: none; font-weight: 500; border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(130, 202, 255, 0.3);">
         Hydrophone MATLAB Post-process Jobs
      </a>
    </li>
    
    <li style="margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <a href="https://wiki.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/DP/pages/21758104/7" 
         style="color: #82caff; text-decoration: none; font-weight: 500; border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(130, 202, 255, 0.3);">
         Data Product: Audio Data (7)
      </a>
	</li>

	<li style="margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <a href="https://wiki.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/DP/pages/46990690/51" 
         style="color: #82caff; text-decoration: none; font-weight: 500; border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(130, 202, 255, 0.3);">
         Data Product: Hydrophone Spectral Probability Density (51)
      </a>
    </li>

	<li style="margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <a href="https://wiki.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/DP/pages/78414511/146" 
         style="color: #82caff; text-decoration: none; font-weight: 500; border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(130, 202, 255, 0.3);">
         Data Product: Spectrogram For Hydrophone Viewer (146)
      </a>
    </li>

    <li style="margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <a href="https://wiki.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/DP/pages/78414074/128" 
         style="color: #82caff; text-decoration: none; font-weight: 500; border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(130, 202, 255, 0.3);">
         Data Product: Hydrophone Acceleration Data (128)
      </a>
    </li>

    <li style="margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <a href="https://wiki.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/DP/pages/42174569/45" 
         style="color: #82caff; text-decoration: none; font-weight: 500; border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(130, 202, 255, 0.3);">
         Data Product: Hydrophone Spectral Data (45)
      </a>
    </li>

    <li style="margin-bottom: 10px;">
      <a href="https://wiki.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/DP/pages/21758026/4" 
         style="color: #82caff; text-decoration: none; font-weight: 500; border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(130, 202, 255, 0.3);">
         Data Product: Log File (4)
      </a>
    </li>

  </ul>
</div>
```
